planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Blue Moon Woodland Phlox spreads slowly by stolons to form loose, semi-evergreen mats in partially shaded areas. Bloom time is April–May in zone 9 — a valuable window when shade garden color options are limited. After blooming, trim plants lightly to maintain neat foliage and stimulate fresh growth. In zone 9 plants may benefit from afternoon shade protection during the hottest summer months; provide consistent moisture to prevent summer dormancy. The notched petals distinguish this species from garden phlox (P. paniculata).

directions: Plant in partial shade to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Blue Moon Woodland Phlox is a genuine shade perennial, growing naturally in the dappled shade of deciduous forest understories; Blue Moon is a cultivar of Wild Blue Phlox (Phlox divaricata), a native eastern North American woodland perennial that provides one of the most delicate and refined spring floral displays available for shaded garden situations. The loose clusters of fragrant, lavender-blue to violet-blue flowers appear in April–May on semi-upright, slightly sticky, hairy stems above the semi-evergreen foliage — the combination of color, fragrance, and delicacy creating a wildflower charm that is difficult to match with non-native spring shade perennials. Blue Moon was selected for deeper, more saturated blue-violet color than typical wild-collected P. divaricata, which tends toward pale lavender — the named cultivar's richer color provides more visual impact in shaded settings where color intensity is often difficult to achieve. As a native species adapted to eastern North American woodland conditions, Wild Blue Phlox in zone 9 provides early spring color in the specific shaded, woodland-edge conditions where it evolved — under deciduous oaks, in the partial shade of north-facing slopes, and in the sheltered pockets that mimic its natural forest understory habitat.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Phlox divaricata Native Plant Profile
North Carolina State University Extension — Phlox divaricata for Southern Shade Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Phlox divaricata 'Blue Moon' Cultivar

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zones 3–7 plant February–May. Hot Lips Turtlehead is best suited for zones 3–8 where cool summers allow the plant to develop fully; in zone 8 with afternoon shade and consistent moisture it can persist as a short-lived perennial. Requires consistently moist to wet soil — it naturally grows along stream banks, pond margins, and in wet woodland clearings. Provide afternoon shade in the southern end of its range. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become crowded and flower production declines.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil — Hot Lips Turtlehead requires more moisture than almost any other commonly grown perennial, thriving in the wet soil conditions along stream banks and pond edges where other perennials struggle; Hot Lips is a cultivar of Pink Turtlehead (Chelone lyonii), a native Appalachian perennial producing distinctive, hooded, two-lipped flowers in rich deep pink to rose-pink — the tubular, pinched flower shape resembling a turtle's head or a closed mouth with two pursed lips gives the entire genus its common name. The unique flower structure is not merely descriptive — the narrow, hooded tube admits only bumblebees (one of the few insects strong enough to pry open the partially closed flower and reach the nectar), making turtlehead a specialist bumblebee plant of considerable ecological importance. The late summer to fall bloom time (August–September) fills a critical timing gap in the late-season perennial border, providing fresh, vivid color when many summer perennials have peaked and fall asters have not yet begun. Hot Lips' compact selection (shorter stems, less flopping than the straight species) makes it more suitable for garden use than wild-collected plants, with the rich pink color being more consistent and saturated than typical species variation.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Chelone lyonii Native Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Turtlehead for Moist Woodland Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Chelone lyonii 'Hot Lips' Profile

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Profusion Beautyberry flowers on new wood; cut plants back hard to 12–18 inches in late winter to promote maximum new growth and fruit production. Without this annual rejuvenation pruning, plants become open and leggy. The spectacular violet-purple berry clusters — tightly packed in rings along the stems — appear September–November when most other shrubs have finished fruiting. The berries persist after leaf drop, showing most dramatically against bare winter stems. Berries attract bluebirds, robins, and cedar waxwings.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Profusion Beautyberry is adaptable to a range of light conditions and performs well in the partial shade typical of zone 9 landscapes under the filtered canopy of high-branched trees; Profusion is a cultivar of Chinese Beautyberry (Callicarpa bodinieri), producing one of the most spectacular and unusual fall fruiting displays of any landscape shrub — clusters of intensely violet-purple to magenta berries packed tightly in whorls along the length of the arching stems in September through November. The color of the berries is genuinely unusual — a saturated, electric, jewel-like violet-purple that has no equivalent among common native berrying shrubs, creating a fall display that stops observers who are unfamiliar with the plant. Profusion was selected for particularly heavy berry production compared to the straight species, bearing abundant clusters on vigorous, arching stems that achieve maximum visual impact in full fruit. The fall berry display works well in combination with the native American Beautyberry (Callicarpa americana), whose berries are a slightly different, more pink-magenta shade — the two species together providing a visual study in the range of violet-purple available within the Callicarpa genus. As a wildlife plant, beautyberry berries are consumed by at least 40 bird species across the US, making it a high-value habitat shrub for landscape designs focused on bird support.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Callicarpa bodinieri 'Profusion' Award of Garden Merit
University of Georgia Extension — Beautyberry Cultivars for Southern Gardens
North Carolina State University Extension — Callicarpa bodinieri Plant Profile

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. The 'Improved' designation refers to the virus-indexed, certified clean version released by UC Davis following the discovery that the original Meyer lemon carried citrus tristeza virus — all nursery-sold Meyer lemons are now this certified clean selection. Meyer lemon is the most cold-hardy of all true lemons, tolerating temperatures to about 22–24°F — borderline zone 8b/9a. In zone 9 it produces fruit through winter (harvest November–March), providing fresh lemons when the tree is at peak ornamental interest with simultaneous flowers and fruit. Grow in containers in colder zones to move indoors when freezes threaten.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil — Meyer lemon requires excellent drainage, regular deep watering during the growing season, and full sun for maximum fruit production; Improved Meyer Lemon is the most widely grown home lemon tree in the United States — a hybrid between a true lemon (Citrus limon) and a mandarin or sweet orange (possibly Citrus sinensis) that produces fruit with significantly less acidity and more sweetness than commercial lemons, combined with a distinctively rich, complex, floral lemon fragrance that makes it the preferred fresh lemon for culinary applications requiring the full flavor profile rather than pure acidity. The thin, smooth, deep yellow to orange-yellow skin is edible and aromatic, zesting beautifully for baking applications where the combination of lemon oil and zest from a Meyer produces a more complex, rounded result than commercial Eureka or Lisbon lemons. The combination of cold hardiness (extending the range further north than most citrus), compact tree size (6–10 feet), heavy and recurrent fruit production, beautiful flowers, and superior culinary quality has made Meyer lemon the definitive home citrus tree across zones 8–11.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Meyer Lemon Home Garden Guide
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Meyer Lemon Production for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Citrus Cultivar Selection for Florida

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–June. Lemon basil matures and begins bolting earlier than sweet basil — pinch flower buds regularly to extend the leaf harvest. In zone 9 it thrives in the summer heat and high humidity that sometimes stresses sweet basil with fungal issues; lemon basil's somewhat different metabolism makes it a useful alternative for summer herb gardens. The fresh lemon fragrance is particularly strong when leaves are pinched or bruised. Plants self-seed freely in warm conditions — allow a few plants to set seed for volunteer seedlings in subsequent seasons.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Lemon Basil is a hybrid basil (Ocimum basilicum x O. americanum) producing smaller, narrower leaves with a distinctive, intense lemon-citrus fragrance and flavor that is completely different from the clove-anise profile of sweet basil — substituting citrus brightness for the classic basil complexity. The lemon flavor is genuine and strong, derived from citral and other lemon-fragrance compounds in the leaf oils, making lemon basil specifically useful in preparations where lemon and herb flavors are desired simultaneously: Thai cuisine (where it is a traditional herb in certain dishes), lemon herb sauces, seafood seasoning, herbed rice, and tea. The compact, fine-textured leaves and upright, branching habit create a smaller, more refined plant than sweet basil — typically 18–24 inches tall with many small branches each tipped with the small, narrow, lemon-scented leaves. Lemon basil is one of the most immediately identifiable herb plants in the garden for visitors unfamiliar with it — the combination of basil's visual character and the unexpected lemon fragrance when leaves are touched creates a memorable sensory experience that makes it a particularly effective plant for teaching about herb gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Varieties for Florida Herb Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Culinary Basil Species and Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Herb Production for Gulf South Home Gardens
